name: Miajadas — circular from Don Benito
description: Cycle the difficult 55.2-mile (88.8 km) Miajadas — circular from Don Benito route, gaining 979 feet (298 metres) through Extremadura's diver
region_intro: Touring cycling routes around Don Benito offer a diverse range of landscapes in Extremadura, Spain. The area features a mix of historical sites, rural countryside, and varied terrain, including both paved and unpaved surfaces. Natural attractions like the Natural Park of Santa Natalia and the Finca de la Serrezuela, with its oak and Mediterranean forest, provide scenic backdrops for rides. The region also includes rehabilitated river areas such as the Charcas del Guadiana, offering additional points of interest. Best touring cycling routes around Don Benito The most popular touring cycling route is Camino Romano and Mozárabe: Don Benito-Medellín Loop, a 13.7 miles (22.0 km) trail that takes 1 hour 16 minutes to complete. This easy route connects Don Benito with historical sites and rural landscapes. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Medellín y Santa Amalia — circular desde Don Benito, a moderate 29.5 miles (47.5 km) path. This route features varied terrain, including both paved and unpaved sections. Local touring cyclists also love the Embalse de Orellana — circular desde Don Benito, a 54.9 miles (88.5 km) trail leading through the wider Extremadura countryside, often completed in about 5 hours 30 minutes. Touring cycling around Don Benito is defined by historical sites, rural landscapes, and granite rock formations. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to more challenging rides. The routes in Don Benito are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 3 reviews. More than 200 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Don Benito's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Don Benito?
A: Based on komoot data, there are over 40 touring cycling routes around Don Benito, offering a wide range of options for exploration across Extremadura, Spain.

Q: What are the different difficulty levels for touring cycling routes in Don Benito?
A: Don Benito offers a diverse range of routes suitable for various skill levels. You'll find 8 easy routes, 19 moderate routes, and 15 difficult routes, ensuring there's something for everyone from beginners to experienced cyclists.

Q: When is the best time of year to go touring cycling in Don Benito?
A: The months of February and September are particularly popular for cycling in the Don Benito area, offering pleasant weather conditions for exploring the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any circular touring cycling routes in Don Benito?
A: Yes, many routes around Don Benito are circular, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1327321778/medellin-y-santa-amalia-circular-desde-don-benito" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Medellín y Santa Amalia — circular desde Don Benito&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 29.5 miles (47.5 km) and features varied terrain.

Q: What kind of natural features can I expect to see along the cycling routes?
A: The region around Don Benito boasts diverse natural features. You can encounter the oak and Mediterranean forest ecosystem at Finca de la Serrezuela, or explore the rehabilitated Charcas del Guadiana, which are leisure areas along the Guadiana River. The Natural Park of Santa Natalia, about 15 km from Don Benito, features a waterfall that is particularly impressive during the rainy season and a historic arched bridge over the Guadámez River.

Q: Are there any historical sites or landmarks to visit while cycling?
A: Absolutely. Many routes connect historical sites and rural landscapes. For instance, the easy &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1327124924/camino-romano-and-mozarabe-don-benito-medellin-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Romano and Mozárabe: Don Benito-Medellín Loop&lt;/a&gt; takes you through historical areas. You can also find several castles and historical sites nearby, such as &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3035140" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magacela Castle&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5725452" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Roman Theater and Castle of Medellín&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy most about cycling in Don Benito?
A: The touring cycling routes in Don Benito are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars from 3 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse terrain, from historical paths to rural landscapes, and the variety of options for different ability levels.

Q: Are there any long-distance touring cycling routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, routes like the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1327221687/embalse-de-orellana-circular-desde-don-benito" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Embalse de Orellana — circular desde Don Benito&lt;/a&gt; offer a substantial ride. This route covers 54.9 miles (88.5 km) through the wider Extremadura countryside and typically takes about 5 hours 30 minutes to complete.

Q: Is there a route that connects Don Benito with other towns or natural paths?
A: Yes, the Vereda de Don Benito a Villanueva de la Serena is a 4.3 km long trail that utilizes an old railway line, connecting Don Benito with the Natural Path of Villanueva de la Serena. This provides a pleasant option for cyclists and nature enthusiasts.

Q: Are there any routes that explore the local countryside and villages?
A: Certainly. The route &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38169120" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Don Benito – La Haba loop from Don Benito&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 41.4 km (25.7 miles) ride that takes you through the local countryside, offering a good way to experience the rural charm of the area.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on the touring cycling routes?
A: The cycling paths around Don Benito feature a mix of paved and unpaved surfaces, offering varied experiences. You'll encounter everything from easy loops through Extremadura's countryside to more challenging rides with significant elevation changes.
tips:
Don Benito is a city that surprises both visitors and residents. The historic center, with its Plaza de España, the Church of Santiago Apóstol, and Renaissance buildings, attracts visitors.
Outstanding museums such as the Ethnographic Museum (rated 4.6/5) and the Automobile Museum are well-received.
Urban parks like Tierno Galván, the Palmeral, and Las Albercas offer green spaces appreciated by tourists and locals alike.
The great parish church of Santiago de Miajadas is an impressive monument. The origin of the religious site dates back to 1348 although the church you see today was built later in the Middle Ages.
Beautiful place in the surroundings of the Ruecas River, near the Palazuelo district. Parallel to the river runs the Vía Verde de las Vegas del Guadiana and las Villuercas.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Miajadas — circular from Don Benito cycling route?
faqs.answer: This is a difficult touring bicycle route, requiring a very good level of fitness. With a distance of nearly 89 kilometers and around 300 meters of elevation gain, it's best suited for experienced cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this route?
faqs.answer: The route is primarily on paved surfaces, which is typical for touring cycling. However, be prepared for some segments that may be unpaved and challenging, potentially requiring you to push your bike at times.

faqs.question: What are the best times of year to cycle the Miajadas — circular from Don Benito route?
faqs.answer: The Extremadura region, where this route is located, is particularly beautiful and verdant in spring. This makes it an ideal time for cycling. Avoid the peak summer months when temperatures can be very high.

faqs.question: Are there any notable landmarks or points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route offers a chance to see some local highlights. You'll pass through the town of Don Benito, and you can also spot the Church of Santiago de Miajadas. Further along, you'll encounter the Ruecas River in Palazuelo.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Miajadas — circular from Don Benito route?
faqs.answer: As the route is circular and starts from Don Benito, you can typically find parking within the town itself. Look for public parking areas in Don Benito to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Is the Miajadas — circular from Don Benito route suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is classified as difficult and requires a very good level of fitness. It is not recommended for beginners or families with young children due to its length, elevation, and potentially challenging unpaved sections.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Miajadas — circular from Don Benito cycling route?
faqs.answer: While there are no specific restrictions against dogs on this public cycling route, it's important to consider the length and difficulty. If you bring a dog, ensure it is well-conditioned for such a long ride and always keep it on a leash, especially near roads or populated areas.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay entrance fees to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Miajadas — circular from Don Benito route, as it uses public roads and paths.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through towns like Don Benito and Miajadas, where you can find various cafes, restaurants, and shops to refuel. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially for the longer stretches between towns.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other official trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route briefly intersects with several official trails. You'll find sections that overlap with the Camino Mozárabe por Trujillo (6%), Camino Visigodo - Etapa 2 (4%), and Vía Verde de las Vegas del Guadiana y las Villuercas (2%), among others.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a touring bicycle ride on this route?
faqs.answer: Given the route's difficulty and length, essential items include plenty of water, energy snacks, a repair kit for your bike, a helmet, appropriate cycling attire, sun protection, and a fully charged phone. Consider carrying a first-aid kit as well.
